pair_convention.spot_explanation: NZD/JPY at 93.62 means 1 NZD buys 93.62 JPY. A rising NZD/JPY rate means NZD is strengthening against JPY (pair moves higher); a falling rate means NZD is weakening against JPY (pair moves lower). Bulls want the pair to rise; bears want the pair to fall.
pair_convention.bullish_means: pair RISES (NZD strengthens vs JPY — pair moves higher)
pair_convention.bearish_means: pair FALLS (NZD weakens vs JPY — pair moves lower)
pair_convention.quote_dossier_tactical_stance: Bullish JPY (net score +0.359, falling USD/JPY = rising JPY)

executive_summary: NZD/JPY has risen approximately 9.4% over the past year, with the pair trading at 93.62 as of the valuation date — down roughly 1.1% over the past month as the US-Iran conflict drove simultaneous risk-off selling of the high-beta NZD and safe-haven buying of the JPY, delivering a double-body blow to the pair. The pair, often termed the 'Kiwi-Yen,' remains a premier barometer for global risk appetite, navigating in February/March 2026 a volatile landscape defined by diverging monetary paths and shifting trade alliances. The dominant bilateral narrative is a profound directional conflict: the NZD dossier's two primary bearish factors (dovish RBNZ at 2.25%, hostile risk environment) and the JPY dossier's two primary bullish factors (BoJ normalisation path to 1.00%, safe-haven demand) are additive headwinds for NZD/JPY — producing a pair that faces pressure from both legs simultaneously. The bilateral rate differential of +150bps (NZD over JPY) is historically thin and narrowing, as the BoJ hikes while the RBNZ holds; this is the clearest structural headwind for the pair. NZD/JPY's extreme risk sensitivity means the Iran conflict has already extracted disproportionate punishment — the pair fell from above 100 in early January 2026 to 93.62 — yet the PPP signal for NZD/JPY is a rare case where NZD is expensive relative to JPY (+10.2% deviation), providing a medium-term fundamental ceiling. Our tactical stance is Bearish NZD/JPY at Conviction 3 (pair falls) and our strategic stance is Bearish NZD/JPY at Conviction 3 (pair falls further), making this one of the most directionally aligned bear cases in the NZD cross complex. The key bilateral tension is whether the March 17-19 Super Week events resolve in a risk-on fashion sufficient to provide a temporary bounce before the structural forces reassert.

market_context.valuation_assessment: At 93.62, NZD/JPY sits +10.2% above PPP fair value of ~85.00 — NZD is one of the few G10 currencies where the PPP signal is bearish versus JPY. This contrasts with JPY's all-time BIS REER low of 67.73 (0th percentile), making JPY deeply structurally cheap. The bilateral REER gap is extreme: NZD's BIS REER at 99.5 (40th percentile) vs JPY's 67.73 (0th percentile) means the yen is approximately 32% undervalued on a real effective basis relative to the kiwi. This valuation asymmetry is the most important strategic anchor for the pair. It is not tactically actionable — REER gaps can persist for years — but it defines the medium-term gravitational pull toward ~85-88.

market_context.recent_regime: NZD/JPY has been in a risk-led correction since breaching 100 in early January 2026. The Iran conflict from February 28 delivered the pair's most severe test, with NZD/JPY attempting to build on a brief bullish breakout but lacking follow-through, and the next meaningful move appearing to hinge less on domestic developments and more on swings in global risk appetite and external drivers. The pair acts as a pure risk barometer: the Nikkei's -8.5% drawdown from its February ATH, VIX spiking to 35, and NZD's high-beta vulnerability combined to produce a 7+ big figure decline from the 100+ peak to current levels around 93.62. Despite the correction, NZD/JPY is still elevated relative to PPP and still at the 93rd percentile of the 52-week range, suggesting the corrective phase has more room to run on fundamental grounds.

bilateral_carry_analysis.carry_assessment: The NZD/JPY carry trade is fragile and deteriorating. The 150bps carry was attractive when the rate gap was stable and volatility was sub-9% (pre-Iran); at current elevated vol (11.5%), the carry-to-vol ratio has dropped to 0.13 — the lowest since 2020. The BoJ is hiking while the RBNZ holds, meaning this carry is structurally compressing: if the BoJ hikes to 1.00% by July while the RBNZ remains at 2.25%, the differential narrows to 125bps. If BoJ reaches 1.25% by year-end, the differential falls to 100bps — the narrowest since 2016. The era of easy profits from carry trades is facing its most significant stress test since the sharp bout of market volatility linked to yen carry unwinding in 2024. NZD/JPY bulls are effectively running carry risk with minimal carry income relative to the vol they are accepting. The forward curve is pricing a modest NZD/JPY decline consistent with the rate differential — but the fundamental base case sees the pair declining materially more than the forwards imply.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The NZD/JPY rate differential of +150bps (RBNZ 2.25% vs BoJ 0.75%) is the narrowest since November 2022 and on a structural compression path — the BoJ is the only G10 central bank hiking while the RBNZ is on hold. Tactically, the March 18 meetings of both the FOMC and the BoJ will not resolve the differential directionally in the near term: the BoJ holds at 95% probability, and the RBNZ meets on April 8 with a similarly near-certain hold. However, the trajectory of the differential is what matters — and it is moving against NZD/JPY. The RBNZ's published OCR track of 2.38% by Q4 2026 implies less than one hike, while the BoJ OIS market prices ~60% probability of a hike by April to 1.00%. The NZ-Japan 10Y sovereign spread at +252bps (4.70% minus 2.18%) remains wide but is also narrowing as JGB yields rise; NZ 10Y at 4.70% vs JGB at 2.18% — a spread that has compressed approximately 20bps over the past month as JGB yields rose on Shunto/wage expectations. The policy rate differential of 150bps is the weakest theoretical carry argument for NZD/JPY in several years. The carry-to-vol ratio of 0.13 is insufficient to attract incremental carry longs at current volatility.

tactical.what_is_priced: The market prices a static 150bps differential through Q2 with gradual BoJ normalisation. The mispricing for NZD/JPY bears is that the BoJ's April/June hike probability is underweighted relative to the combination of strong Shunto results and above-target core-core CPI. A surprise BoJ hike at the April 27-28 meeting would compress the differential to 125bps within the tactical window — NZD/JPY negative.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic rate differential path is unambiguously NZD/JPY bearish. The BoJ is expected to hike from 0.75% to 1.00-1.25% by year-end while the RBNZ remains at 2.25% or hikes by only one 25bp step to 2.50% by September. This narrows the bilateral differential from +150bps to +100-125bps — a 25-50bps compression. Meanwhile, the NZ-JP 10Y sovereign spread is also compressing as JGB yields rise faster than NZ bond yields. Historically, NZD/JPY has a tight relationship with this 10Y spread; a 30bps compression in the spread is associated with approximately 3-5 big figures of NZD/JPY decline. The key strategic risk to the bearish rate thesis is the scenario where NZ inflation reaccelerates (April 17 Q1 CPI above 3.2%) and forces the RBNZ to hike twice (to 2.75%) before year-end — this would slow the differential compression but not reverse it, as the BoJ path toward 1.25% remains intact. The Fed's expected cuts of 1-2 by September create an additional USD weakness headwind, but for NZD/JPY this primarily works through the cross rather than directly.

factors.horizon_split: Both horizons are bearish with increasing confidence over time. Strategic confidence rises to 72 because the combination of BoJ hikes and RBNZ on hold becomes more certain over 6 months, and the carry unwind dynamics are non-linear — the longer the position accumulates, the more vulnerable to sudden unwinding.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral inflation/growth picture is modestly NZD/JPY bearish on net. NZ GDP at +1.3% YoY and Japan at +1.1% — growth differential is narrow (+0.2pp NZ advantage) and not a directional driver. However, the more important bilateral signal comes from comparing the two economies' inflation dynamics and their central bank implications. Japan's wage acceleration — base pay at +3.0% YoY, the strongest since 1992 — is the most bullish domestic data in the JPY dossier and the single most important upcoming catalyst (Shunto on March 12). The NZ ANZ Business Outlook survey confirms pricing intentions at a net 53.3% and inflation expectations moving higher to 2.93% year ahead, with cost pressure expectations at 79.4%. Paradoxically, NZ's rising inflationary pressure is not yet a catalyst for NZD/JPY bulls because the RBNZ has not responded — and it won't until the April 17 CPI. Meanwhile, Japan's wage data is directly actionable for the BoJ on March 12 (Shunto) and April 27. The asymmetry is significant: Japan's inflation data is already feeding into BoJ action, while NZ's inflation data has a 6-8 week lag before RBNZ action. Japan's unemployment at 2.7% (near full employment) vs NZ at 5.4% (decade high) creates a stark labour market differential that suppresses any NZD wage inflation narrative.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the growth and inflation dynamics become more balanced from a bilateral perspective. NZ's superior dairy-led recovery (IMF forecasts 2.7% GDP for 2026) versus Japan's more modest 1.0% projection creates a widening growth gap that would normally support NZD/JPY. However, Japan's wage-price cycle, if self-sustaining as Shunto results suggest, is qualitatively more important for monetary policy normalisation than NZ's growth recovery — which is commodity-driven and may not force RBNZ action as aggressively. The NZ CPI-BoJ normalisation timing asymmetry is the key strategic swing factor: if NZ Q1 CPI (April 17) beats and RBNZ signals tightening at May 27 MPS, while the BoJ hikes in April, the differential compression slows from its current pace and NZD/JPY finds a floor around 88-90. The strategic neutral reflects genuine two-sidedness: bullish growth for NZ (dairy, tourism, recovery) vs bullish inflation for Japan (wages, normalisation).

tactical.bilateral_analysis: NZD/JPY is the archetype risk-on/risk-off G10 pair. NZD is the highest-beta risk-on currency in G10 (correlation to S&P 500: ~+0.85-0.95; VIX beta: ~-0.80); JPY is the primary G10 safe-haven currency (negative VIX correlation: ~-0.75). The bilateral combination produces the strongest risk-sentiment beta in the entire G10 FX universe — NZD/JPY moves approximately 1.5-2x the magnitude of either component pair in isolation when VIX spikes. The Iran conflict has been decisive: the VIX spiked from ~16.75 to 35 on March 9 before settling at ~24, and NZD/JPY fell from 100+ in January to 93.62 in early March — a ~6.5 big figure decline driven almost entirely by risk sentiment. The S&P 500 remains below its 100-day MA and the Nikkei is down 8.5% from its ATH — both cross-asset signals that historically predict further NZD/JPY weakness. The Nikkei correlation is particularly important: when Japanese equities sell off, Japanese investors repatriate foreign income (buying JPY) while simultaneously NZD weakens as risk appetite falls — creating a perfect bilateral storm. The near-term risk environment remains hostile: VIX at 24 is still at the 80th percentile of the 1-year range, Super Week (March 17-19) creates binary event risk, and the US government faces a shutdown deadline.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, risk appetite normalisation is the base case (60% probability) as the Iran conflict resolves and VIX returns toward 15-18. In a risk-on recovery, NZD/JPY would snap back sharply — NZD's high beta means it outperforms in recoveries as much as it underperforms in drawdowns. However, the risk sentiment factor becomes neutralised over 6 months because the fundamental rate differential and carry unwind dynamics become the dominant driver as BoJ hikes accumulate. The JPY dossier's strategic view rightly notes that the Nikkei correction from 59,332 to 54,248 (-8.5%) looks like a normal bull market correction (IG credit near 30-year tights confirms no systemic stress), but JPY tends to strengthen on BoJ hikes even in risk-on environments — weakening the usual NZD/JPY risk-on beta. The strategic neutral reflects genuine uncertainty: the pair's risk sensitivity works both ways, and over 6 months fundamental forces dominate sentiment.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The bilateral flow picture is NZD/JPY bearish on net, but with a critical caveat — the NZD short squeeze risk creates significant tactical noise. On the bearish side: Japanese fiscal year-end flows (March 31) are the most powerful near-term bilateral flow driver. As documented in the JPY dossier, Japanese institutions historically repatriate foreign income and settle FX obligations in the final 3-4 weeks of March, generating documented yen demand of 300-500 pips against the dollar — and a proportional amount against NZD. For NZD/JPY specifically, Japanese investors who hold NZ bonds or NZD-denominated assets (attracted by the positive carry) may repatriate into yen ahead of March 31, creating bilateral selling pressure on NZD/JPY beyond the simple USD/JPY channel. The life insurer underhedging at 14-year lows (44.4-45.7% hedge ratios) represents a structural forced-buying of JPY if any JPY appreciation is triggered. On the bullish side: one of the most popular investment and trading strategies has been the yen carry trade, where investors borrow the Japanese yen and sell it for high-yield currencies such as NZD, AUD, and GBP. NZD is specifically a traditional yen-carry destination. The NZD leg's extreme short positioning (85th percentile) creates squeeze potential that could temporarily push the pair higher if Super Week delivers risk-on catalysts.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic flow picture is dominated by the structural JPY carry unwind dynamic. When the carry trade was unwound, as for example in late 2008, NZD and AUD depreciated rapidly against the Japanese yen. The July 2024 carry unwind analogue is more recent and directly comparable — NZD/JPY fell approximately 15 big figures in weeks. As the BoJ hikes from 0.75% to 1.00-1.25%, the incentive for Japanese investors to fund positions in NZD assets diminishes at the margin. Japanese direct investment in NZ stands at NZ$8.828 billion — these positions include currency risk that becomes increasingly expensive to maintain as JPY strengthens and carry diminishes. New Zealand's total exports to Japan were US$2.32 billion during 2024 (UN COMTRADE), representing structural NZD selling flow from Japanese importers of NZ goods. Overall, exports from New Zealand to Japan grew strongly in the first half of 2025, up 5.3% on an annual basis — this bilateral trade growth means the natural flow from NZ exporters receiving JPY and converting to NZD is modest and insufficient to offset carry unwind dynamics.

tactical.contrarian_case: The contrarian case for NZD/JPY longs is modest. NZD net shorts at the 85th percentile represent squeeze potential, but the JPY leg is not providing a contrarian bullish signal (only at the 45th percentile of positioning). A pure NZD positioning squeeze would likely drive NZD/USD toward 0.6050 but NZD/JPY would benefit less than NZD/USD if the squeeze is triggered by a dovish FOMC (which would also strengthen JPY via the safe-haven/rate channel). The cleanest contrarian signal for NZD/JPY bulls requires: (1) NZD short cover AND (2) JPY shorts rebuilding simultaneously — the latter requires Iran de-escalation and VIX falling below 18. This combination is possible (25% probability) but not the base case.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The commodity and terms-of-trade netting for NZD/JPY produces a clear bearish signal for the pair. For oil (Brent ~$87.80/bbl, spiked to $119.50/bbl): NZD gets a mild negative (-1 to -0.5) as an oil importer; JPY gets a strong negative as THE most energy-dependent G10 economy. However, the asymmetry is important: Japan imports 87% of its energy (90-94% of crude from the Middle East, ~70% via Hormuz), making Japan's ToT shock from $100+ oil catastrophically worse than NZ's. The net commodity effect for NZD/JPY from oil rising is mildly NZD/JPY positive (JPY weakens more on oil spikes). For dairy prices (GDT index +18% YTD): NZD gets a strong positive; JPY gets a negligible effect — net strongly NZD/JPY positive. However, the Japan-specific energy vulnerability is the dominant ToT factor in the current regime because the oil price spike is an active and acute force. Japan's ToT are acutely negative: the January trade deficit was ¥1,152.7B and Brent above $90/bbl is structurally widening Japan's import bill by ¥2-3T per $10/bbl. NZ's dairy windfall (GDT +18% YTD, terms of trade +3.7% QoQ) is a real but smaller bilateral ToT positive for NZD. The net bilateral assessment is moderately NZD/JPY bearish because the oil impact structurally benefits JPY more by weakening it less than NZD (both are oil importers, but Japan's dependency is catastrophically greater and already priced into JPY weakness — meaning the marginal benefit to NZD/JPY from oil above $90 is diminishing), while dairy's NZD benefit is partially offset by NZ also importing crude.

If oil retraces toward $75-80 and dairy sustains above GDT 1,450, the bilateral ToT would swing NZD/JPY positive by approximately 150-200 pips.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: Strategically, the ToT picture for NZD/JPY becomes genuinely two-sided. The NZ dairy structural advantage (NZ-China FTA duty-free access vs 125% US tariffs, NZ$14B Fonterra income) strengthens NZD's ToT position over 6 months, but Japan's primary income surplus of ¥41.59T (2025 record) provides a structural current account floor under the yen that the trade deficit cannot overwhelm. Japan's external position is net NIIP of $3,663B (world's largest creditor nation) — this dwarfs NZ's current account deficit of -3.1% to -4.0% of GDP. The bilateral comparison is stark: Japan is the world's largest creditor providing a structural floor under JPY regardless of ToT shocks; NZ has a current account deficit that makes it structurally dependent on capital flows. However, NZ's dairy tailwind is unique in the G10 — no comparable commodity windfall exists for Japan. NZ exported US$2.32 billion to Japan in 2024, with a significant dairy component. If dairy prices sustain above GDT 1,450, NZ's CA deficit narrows toward -3.0% and Japan's CA surplus remains structurally massive — the bilateral CA comparison remains Japan-favourable but NZ's deterioration arrested. Dairy's strong positive for NZD dominates when oil/energy spike is priced in, but Japan's NIIP and CA surplus creates a structural resilience. Net effect is approximately neutral for NZD/JPY over 6 months — dairy benefit for NZD roughly offset by Japan's massive NIIP generating primary income flows.

tactical.bilateral_analysis: The guidance divergence between the RBNZ and BoJ is unambiguously NZD/JPY bearish. The RBNZ under Governor Breman has delivered an explicitly dovish hold — OCR track of 2.38% by Q4 2026, language emphasising patience and global uncertainty, and a policy framework that requires CPI to return to the 2% midpoint before any tightening. The BoJ under Ueda has moved from cautious to explicitly hawkish in its communication: Board member Takata called the conditions 'a true dawn,' Ueda described upcoming meetings as 'live,' and the January Summary of Opinions doubled mentions of 'weak yen' concerns. The credibility differential is notable: the RBNZ publishes its OCR track and delivers within 25-50bp historically (high transparency, dovish priced in precisely); the BoJ is more opaque but has consistently delivered on the normalisation path since January 2024. The upcoming March 18 BoJ statement will be scrutinised for language changes regarding the Iran conflict. Any maintenance of the hawkish tone despite Iran caution would be NZD/JPY bearish. The RBNZ does not meet until April 8, so there is no offsetting guidance event in the tactical window.

strategic.bilateral_analysis: The strategic guidance picture maintains a bearish NZD/JPY bias but with increasing complexity. The RBNZ's May 27 MPS is the first opportunity for a hawkish guidance shift if Q1 CPI (April 17) surprises upside. If the RBNZ revises its OCR track to 2.75% by Q4 2026, the bearish rate differential story for NZD/JPY weakens. However, even in the NZD-bullish scenario where RBNZ raises its track by 37.5bps, the BoJ is expected to have already hiked once (to 1.00%) — so the bilateral differential compression from 150bps would slow (to perhaps narrowing by only 12.5-25bps instead of 50bps) rather than reversing. Takaichi's nomination of reflationists (Asada and Sato) represents a risk that BoJ normalisation slows after reaching 1.00% — this is the primary downside risk to the bearish NZD/JPY rate thesis strategically, but it doesn't reverse it. The BoJ's institutional independence under Ueda is intact through April 2028.

For NZD/JPY specifically: (1) March 12 Shunto — a 5.5%+ wage result would raise BoJ April hike probability above 80%, pushing NZD/JPY down 100-200 pips; (2) March 17 GDT — a positive dairy result (6th consecutive gain) would provide NZD/JPY support of 50-100 pips; (3) March 18 BoJ statement — hawkish language despite Iran caution would be NZD/JPY negative; (4) March 18-19 NZ GDP — a beat above +0.8% QoQ would provide a 50-100 pip temporary NZD/JPY lift; (5) March 18 FOMC — a dovish outcome (3+ cuts in dot plot) would weaken USD, providing indirect NZD support, but would also be risk-positive reducing JPY safe-haven demand — net effect ambiguous for NZD/JPY but modestly positive (NZD beta to USD risk higher than JPY's). tactical.bilateral_analysis: For a non-USD cross like NZD/JPY, the USD liquidity channel transmits indirectly through two mechanisms: (1) the common USD leg — both NZD/USD and USD/JPY are influenced by DXY and USD funding; (2) the vehicle currency channel — virtually all NZD/JPY liquidity is intermediated through USD. The current USD environment creates a net bearish NZD/JPY signal. USD weakness (DXY ~98.6; 'Sell America' narrative) is NZD-positive via the USD leg, but simultaneously JPY is a greater beneficiary of USD weakness on a risk-adjusted basis because the BoJ-Fed differential compression story is the dominant USD/JPY driver. The Iran conflict has created a paradox: DXY is elevated (safe-haven demand) but the 'Sell America' narrative is also building — DXY at ~99.0 reflects this tension. Japan's fiscal year-end creates acute USD/JPY demand (Japanese entities selling USD assets and buying JPY), which biases USD/JPY lower. The NZD/JPY impact of fiscal year-end flows is that they compress the pair by strengthening JPY faster than they weaken USD. The cross-currency basis for NZD/JPY (estimated at -30 to -60bps for 3M) is slightly wider than the NZD/USD basis, reflecting higher cost to fund NZD vs JPY in the cross-currency market.

Factor 1 (Policy & Rates, weight 0.24) delivers the single largest bearish contribution (-0.168): the RBNZ-BoJ bilateral rate differential of +150bps is the narrowest since 2022 and is on a structural compression path as the BoJ normalises from 0.75% toward 1.00-1.25%. The bilateral-specific factors that modify the standalone views are: (1) Japan's fiscal year-end repatriation flows — documented and historically reliable NZD/JPY selling pressure of 150-300 pips over March 19-31; (2) the carry unwind dynamic specific to JPY-funded NZD positions, which represents one of the most popular currency carry trade strategies — borrowing yen to buy NZD — and is now unwinding as BoJ hiking raises JPY funding costs. strategic.score_interpretation: Net score of -0.278 is moderately bearish NZD/JPY. The strategic score is less extreme than the tactical (-0.278 vs -0.475) because multiple factors (Risk Appetite, External Balance, Event Risk) shift to neutral as geopolitical noise fades and the dairy/growth NZD story asserts. The two factors that dominate strategically are Factor 1 (Policy & Rates, -0.173) and Factor 6 (Guidance & Credibility, -0.078) — together contributing -0.251 of the -0.278 total.
strategic.rationale: The strategic NZD/JPY bearish case rests on two immovable pillars: (1) the unambiguous rate convergence path — BoJ hiking from 0.75% to 1.00-1.25% while the RBNZ holds at 2.25% or manages only a single 25bp hike to 2.50%, compressing the bilateral differential from 150bps toward 100-125bps; and (2) the institutional commitment of both central banks to their respective trajectories, confirmed by multiple rounds of communication with high credibility. Factor 1 alone contributes -0.173 at 0.24 weight — the most powerful single factor in the framework. The interaction between the two dossiers is uniquely directionally reinforcing: the NZD dossier's strategic Bullish NZD thesis (driven by dairy ToT and USD weakness) and the JPY dossier's strategic Bullish JPY thesis (driven by BoJ normalisation and carry unwind) do NOT cancel for NZD/JPY — both factors can be simultaneously correct while the pair still falls, because JPY's strengthening from the BoJ path is more powerful than NZD's strengthening from dairy prices. The bilateral-specific edge lies in the NZD/JPY carry unwind dynamics: borrowing yen and selling it for NZD is one of the most popular carry trade strategies in FX, and as the BoJ hikes, these positions become increasingly expensive to maintain. The path to the strategic target of ¥88.00 by September requires the BoJ to hike once (April or June) and the RBNZ to remain on hold — both are the base case. Key dates: April 27-28 BoJ (60% hike probability = most important catalyst); April 17 NZ Q1 CPI (if below 2.5%, removes NZD upside scenario; if above 3.2%, creates NZD/JPY floor at ¥89-90). Conviction at 3 rather than 4 reflects the genuine upside risk from NZD's dairy-led structural recovery — if the RBNZ hikes twice by September (to 2.75%), the differential compresses by only 12.5bps and the bear case loses force. Factor 6 (Guidance & Credibility) contributes -0.078 at 0.12 weight, reflecting the BoJ's credible normalisation path under Ueda versus the RBNZ's explicitly dovish hold — a divergence that widens over the strategic horizon as BoJ hike cycles accumulate. The strategic scorecard is notable for its concentration: 90% of the bearish weighted score comes from just two factors (Policy & Rates and Guidance), while six other factors are neutral — this reflects a thesis that is narrowly but deeply grounded in monetary policy divergence rather than broadly spread across economic fundamentals. The head-and-shoulders chart pattern (head ~100, neckline ~91.00-91.45, measured move ~82-84) and the BIS REER bilateral gap (NZD 99.5 at 40th percentile vs JPY 67.73 at 0th percentile — the most extreme bilateral REER gap in the modern era) provide the strategic technical and valuation confirmation of the fundamental thesis. The 6M forward-implied rate of ~92.17 vs our expected 88.00 represents a -417 pip quantifiable edge — the basis for strategic bearish conviction.
strategic.g10_relative_ranking: NZD/JPY strategically ranks as the #1 most attractive bearish cross among all NZD pairs, ahead of NZD/USD (which benefits from USD weakness) and NZD/AUD (which has the RBNZ-RBA gap working against but in a different direction). Among all JPY long expressions, NZD/JPY short ranks #2 behind USD/JPY short (which captures the direct BoJ-Fed differential compression) and ahead of EUR/JPY short (where the ECB is pausing rather than cutting). The pair's rank benefits from the pure directional consensus across both legs — both the NZD side (dovish RBNZ, dairy-led but not rate-reactive recovery) and the JPY side (BoJ normalisation, carry unwind) point in the same direction for the cross.

consensus_divergence.why_we_differ: Three bilateral factors not captured in most cross-rate forecasts drive our divergence from retail consensus. First, the NZD/JPY-specific carry unwind dynamic: borrowing JPY to buy NZD is one of the most popular G10 carry strategies, and as the BoJ hikes, this specific position unwinds with greater force than linear arithmetic from standalone NZD/USD and USD/JPY forecasts suggests — the non-linearity of carry unwind is systematically underweighted in model-based forecasts. Second, Japan's fiscal year-end repatriation specifically affects NZD/JPY as Japanese institutional investors repatriate from NZ bond and equity positions (NZ$8.83B cumulative Japanese FDI in NZ) — this bilateral flow dynamic is invisible to USD-centric forecast models. Third, the bilateral PPP overvaluation of NZD vs JPY (+10.2%) is the only major G10 cross where NZD is expensive — most retail forecasters use USD-centric models that miss this bilateral fundamental anchor entirely, instead simply combining NZD/USD and USD/JPY models that separately show NZD cheap vs USD and JPY cheap vs USD.

Strategically, the bearish signal moderates because risk appetite normalisation (Iran resolution), NZ's dairy recovery, and the NZD short squeeze potential create genuine offsets over 6 months even as the rate convergence story remains the dominant driver.

catalysts.rationale: The BoJ is expected to hold at 0.75% (~95% probability), but the statement language is the critical variable. If Ueda maintains the hawkish tone regarding Shunto wages and downplays the Iran conflict impact, markets will price the April hike at 75-80% probability — sending NZD/JPY lower by 100-150 pips. The bilateral impact is asymmetric: a dovish BoJ statement (introducing 'extended monitoring' language) would push NZD/JPY higher by 150-250 pips as JPY safe-haven longs are unwound. The statement's treatment of the Iran conflict vs Shunto wages is the key binary variable for NZD/JPY direction on this date.

catalysts.event: FOMC Decision with SEP and Dot Plot
catalysts.rationale: A dovish FOMC (3+ cuts) weakens USD, supporting NZD/USD (bullish NZD/JPY) but also reduces risk-off USD demand, reducing JPY safe-haven bid (bearish JPY). The net NZD/JPY impact is ambiguous as the two effects partially offset. NZD typically benefits more from USD weakness than JPY loses from reduced safe-haven demand in a risk-on environment, suggesting a dovish FOMC is modestly NZD/JPY positive via the NZD beta. A hawkish FOMC (1 cut only) would be modestly NZD/JPY bearish.

GDP consensus of +0.5-0.7% QoQ. A beat above +1.0% would trigger NZD short covering, providing a 100-150 pip NZD/JPY bounce. A miss (negative QoQ) would confirm recession fears and push NZD/JPY toward 91.00. However, any NZD/JPY gain from GDP beat is capped by the structural JPY strength from BoJ normalisation — the carry unwind story does not pause for an NZ data beat. The asymmetry is that a miss is more NZD/JPY bearish (confirming the dovish RBNZ hold is appropriate and reducing any scenario where RBNZ could tighten) than a beat is bullish (which adds only temporary NZD support without changing the bilateral rate trajectory).

The most predictable and well-documented seasonal pattern in the NZD/JPY calendar. Japanese institutions with NZD-denominated assets (bonds, equities, FDI income from the NZ$8.83B cumulative Japanese FDI in NZ) repatriate income and close FX books. Historical NZD/JPY weakness in the final 7-10 trading days of March is typically 150-300 pips. The effect is independent of risk sentiment — it occurs regardless of the geopolitical backdrop. The bilateral specificity is important: Japanese institutional investors in NZ assets (bonds, equities) must sell NZD and buy JPY to settle books, creating a mechanical NZD/JPY headwind that is distinct from the broad USD/JPY fiscal year-end flow.

The single most important NZD data release in the strategic horizon. An above-3.2% print forces RBNZ tightening expectations forward, reducing the NZD carry disadvantage vs JPY and providing a floor for NZD/JPY at ~89-91. A below-2.5% print validates the disinflation thesis and reopens OCR cut speculation — widening the bilateral disadvantage further and sending NZD/JPY toward 87-88 ahead of the May 27 RBNZ MPS. The market currently prices ~2.5-2.8%, meaning surprises in both directions are possible. This release has the single largest potential impact on the strategic NZD/JPY thesis because it determines whether the rate convergence is one-directional (BoJ hiking alone) or bi-directional (BoJ hiking AND RBNZ potentially following).

The updated Outlook Report would also raise projected rates for FY2026, increasing pressure on the carry trade. A hike at this meeting within the current tactical window (decisions made in mid-March) makes this the primary strategic trigger for position entry decisions. If the BoJ holds at this meeting, the bearish strategic thesis weakens materially but does not reverse.

catalysts.event: RBNZ MPS with Full Forecast Update
catalysts.rationale: The first RBNZ full forecast update following Q1 CPI. If the RBNZ maintains its dovish hold (OCR track 2.38% by Q4 2026), the bilateral rate narrative remains bearish NZD/JPY as the BoJ-RBNZ divergence widens further. If the RBNZ revises its OCR track upward to 2.75%+ by Q4 2026 following a Q1 CPI beat, the bilateral rate narrative shifts from persistently NZD-disadvantaged to converging — providing a floor for NZD/JPY at ~89-91. The combination of a BoJ April hike (to 1.00%) AND an RBNZ May hawkish signal (to 2.75%) would produce a near-neutral differential around 175bps (NZD over JPY on the 10Y), stabilising the pair.

catalysts.rationale: Most likely timing for the first Fed rate cut. A cut would weaken USD, supporting NZD/USD (+100-150 pips) while the BoJ is simultaneously expected to have hiked. The combination of Fed cut + BoJ hike in the same month (BoJ June meeting June 15-16) is the most powerful bilateral catalyst for JPY appreciation in the strategic horizon. NZD benefits from USD weakness but the JPY strength from the BoJ-Fed convergence is more powerful for the NZD/JPY cross — the net effect is modestly bearish NZD/JPY as JPY strengthens more than NZD on this policy divergence event.

signposts.bilateral_importance: The AUD/JPY vs NZD/JPY spread is unique to this pair — it measures the intra-Antipodean divergence vs JPY that captures the RBA-RBNZ rate gap. A widening spread confirms the NZD-specific underperformance vs JPY that makes NZD/JPY the better short than AUD/JPY, validating the bilateral framework's identification of NZD/JPY as the preferred bearish expression among Antipodean-JPY crosses.

assumptions: Spot rate cross-calculation: NZD/JPY at 93.62 is consistent with NZD/USD 0.5930 × USD/JPY 157.90 (actual spot as of March 11, 2026; minor discrepancies from bid/ask spreads in individual dossiers have been reconciled to the pair's own spot as cited in the NZD dossier key crosses table at 93.62).

NZD/JPY implied volatility: No direct NZD/JPY IV data was available from web research. The 11.5% (1M) estimate is derived as a weighted blend of NZD/USD IV (12.5%) and USD/JPY IV (10.0%) less a 1-1.5 vol cross-rate haircut for the correlation between the USD legs, consistent with standard FX cross-vol arithmetic. This estimate carries a ±1 vol uncertainty.

Forward points: NZD/JPY forward points are estimated from the NZD/JPY rate differential (approximately +150bps NZD over JPY) adjusted for the cross-rate basis. Exact NZD/JPY interbank forwards were not sourced directly; the estimates reflect standard carry-based forward arithmetic and may differ from market quotes by ±10-20 pips per tenor.

Bilateral trade flows: Two-way NZ-Japan trade was NZ$9.36bn for the year ending September 2024, with Japan as NZ's 4th/5th largest trading partner. These figures inform the flow analysis but do not dominate the pair dynamics, which are primarily driven by financial flows (carry, portfolio investment) rather than trade settlement flows.

Shunto results (Factors 2 and 7): The March 12 concentrated response day results were not available as of the March 11 valuation date. The analysis uses Rengo's publicly stated demand of 5.85-5.94% as the basis for scenario analysis, with 5.5% as the consensus expectation threshold.

PPP fair value: NZD vs JPY PPP of ~¥85.00 (from NZD dossier: NZD deviation from JPY +10.2%) is derived from OECD data applied to current spot rates. PPP is used as a long-run mean-reversion anchor, not a near-term trading target.

Carry trade structural estimates: The $1-2 trillion estimate for global JPY-funded carry positions is from the JPY dossier and reflects market estimates; the specific NZD/JPY component is not separately quantified and is assumed to be approximately 5-10% of total JPY carry given NZD's historical role as a primary carry destination.
